catastrophizing.label: Catastrophizing
catastrophizing.description: Imagining the worst possible outcome and treating it as inevitable.
catastrophizing.cbt_explanation: You're jumping to the worst-case scenario. In reality, many outcomes are possible, and the worst case is rarely the most likely.
reframe_templates: What's the most likely outcome, not just the worst one?
Has the worst-case scenario actually happened before in similar situations?
If a friend told you this, would you agree it's definitely going to be that bad?
What evidence do you have that this will definitely happen?
Can you think of a time when you expected the worst and it turned out okay?

black_and_white.label: Black & White Thinking
black_and_white.description: Seeing things in only two categories — perfect or failure, with nothing in between.
black_and_white.cbt_explanation: You're thinking in extremes. Most situations exist on a spectrum, and partial success is still success.
reframe_templates: Is there a middle ground between total success and complete failure?
On a scale of 1-10, where does this actually fall?
Would you judge someone else this harshly for the same thing?
What parts went well, even if not everything was perfect?
Can something be 'good enough' without being perfect?

overgeneralization.label: Overgeneralization
overgeneralization.description: Taking one event and assuming it applies to everything, using words like 'always' or 'never'.
overgeneralization.cbt_explanation: You're taking a single event and applying it universally. One instance doesn't create a permanent pattern.
reframe_templates: Is this truly 'always' or 'never', or has there been at least one exception?
Are you basing this on one event or a real pattern with evidence?
Can you name a time when the opposite happened?
What would you say if you replaced 'always' with 'sometimes'?
Is this a fact or a feeling disguised as a fact?

mind_reading.label: Mind Reading
mind_reading.description: Assuming you know what others are thinking, usually something negative about you.
mind_reading.cbt_explanation: You're assuming you know what someone else thinks without checking. We can't read minds — our guesses often reflect our own fears, not reality.
reframe_templates: Do you have actual evidence for what they're thinking, or are you guessing?
Have you asked them directly how they feel?
Could there be another explanation for their behavior?
Are you projecting your own fears onto them?
What would you think if you saw someone do what you did?

fortune_telling.label: Fortune Telling
fortune_telling.description: Predicting the future negatively without evidence.
fortune_telling.cbt_explanation: You're predicting a negative outcome as if it's certain. None of us can see the future — predictions based on fear aren't facts.
reframe_templates: What evidence do you have that this will definitely happen?
Have your negative predictions been accurate in the past?
What's a more balanced prediction based on actual evidence?
If you can't predict good outcomes, why trust bad predictions?
What steps can you take to influence a better outcome?

emotional_reasoning.label: Emotional Reasoning
emotional_reasoning.description: Believing something is true just because you feel it strongly.
emotional_reasoning.cbt_explanation: Your feelings are real, but they aren't always accurate reflections of reality. Feeling scared doesn't mean you're in danger.
reframe_templates: Just because you feel it, does that make it true?
What are the facts, separate from how you feel right now?
Would you feel the same way about this tomorrow or next week?
Has a strong feeling ever led you to a wrong conclusion before?
What would the evidence say if you took emotions out of it?

should_statements.label: Should Statements
should_statements.description: Using rigid rules about how you or others 'should' behave, leading to guilt or frustration.
should_statements.cbt_explanation: You're applying rigid rules to yourself or others. 'Should' creates pressure and guilt. Try replacing it with 'I'd prefer' or 'It would be nice if'.
reframe_templates: What if you replaced 'should' with 'I'd prefer to' or 'it would be nice if'?
Where did this rule come from? Is it truly universal?
Are you holding yourself to a standard you wouldn't expect of others?
What would happen if you didn't follow this 'should'?
Is this expectation realistic given your current circumstances?

labeling.label: Labeling
labeling.description: Attaching a fixed negative label to yourself or others based on a single event.
labeling.cbt_explanation: You're defining yourself by one action or trait. People are complex — one mistake doesn't make you a 'failure', just like one success doesn't make you 'perfect'.
reframe_templates: Is this label the whole truth, or just one part of who you are?
Would you label a friend this way for the same thing?
Can you describe the behavior without the label?
What evidence contradicts this label?
Does one event really define an entire person?

personalization.label: Personalization
personalization.description: Blaming yourself for things outside your control, or taking everything personally.
personalization.cbt_explanation: You're taking responsibility for something that isn't entirely yours to carry. Many factors contribute to outcomes — you're just one of them.
reframe_templates: What other factors contributed to this situation besides you?
Is it really all your fault, or are there other people and circumstances involved?
Would you blame someone else completely if they were in your position?
What percentage of this was actually in your control?
Are you taking on responsibility that isn't yours?

discounting_positive.label: Discounting the Positive
discounting_positive.description: Dismissing good experiences or achievements as if they don't count.
discounting_positive.cbt_explanation: You're filtering out the positive and only keeping the negative. Your achievements and good moments are real — they count too.
reframe_templates: If this happened to someone else, would you dismiss it so easily?
Why does this positive thing 'not count' but negative things do?
Can you sit with this good moment without explaining it away?
What if you accepted this positive experience at face value?
Would someone who cares about you agree that this doesn't matter?

none.label: Balanced Thinking
none.description: No cognitive distortion detected — this reflects healthy, balanced thinking.
